title: M5 iPad Pro vs M3 iPad Air: Which iPad to Buy in 2025?
transcript: This is the M5 iPad Pro and this is the M3 iPad Air. Two of Apple's blockbuster tablets. And today we're breaking down one of the toughest decisions. Should you go for the iPad Pro or the iPad Air? Look, if you're a casual user, a student, or a creator trying to pick the best iPad for your work and budget, then this video is for you. We will compare design, performance, display, [music] accessories, and realworld experience to find out which one actually fits your workflow and lifestyle. So, let's dive right in. All right. First, let's discuss design. Physically, they both look nearly identical. Same flat edges, USBC port, and thin bezels. Also, both are available in 11in and 13-in sizes. However, what's interesting is that Apple usually reserves the Air Moniker for their thinner and lighter devices, and you can see that with the MacBook Air and the new iPhone Air as examples. But what's funny is that the iPad Pro is actually thinner and lighter than the iPad Air. In fact, it's just 5.1 mm thick, making it not just the thinnest iPad that Apple has ever made, but their thinnest product ever. The iPad Air is a bit thicker at 6.1 mm. It's not a dealbreaker at all, as both feel premium and portable. But man, once you get used to holding the iPad Pro, it's almost as if this should have been called the Air. But for students who carry these daily, the difference in backpack weight is minimal. But you will notice the Pro's slimmer feel when you're holding it in your hands. It also just feels and looks a lot more futuristic. But where things differ more with the form factor is if you look at the camera setup. The Air has a single 12 megapixel wide rear camera, whereas the Pro has that plus a LAR scanner with a prograde lens that supports ProRes video and better AR tracking. Personally, I don't think I have even once used the LAR scanner, but if you are an architecture student or a 3D creative using LAR for spatial scanning, then this is a big plus. Otherwise, both will take great photos and scan documents easily. Another thing to note is with the speakers. The iPad Air still only offers two stereo speakers, whereas the iPad Pro features a quad speaker system. The Air sounds great until you start listening from the speakers of the Pro because man, the audio is impressive on the Pro. With the audio and the display of the iPad Pro, it feels like a movie theater right in your hands. And speaking of display, let's talk about that next as it's the part that you look at all day. The iPad Air has a liquid retina LCD display with 500 nits of brightness and a 60 Hz refresh rate. It's sharp, color accurate, and great for reading research or light creative work. But the iPad Pro is next level. It uses Apple's Tandem OLED Ultra Retina XDR display. I know it's quite a mouthful, but this is what you get with it. Deeper blacks, better contrast, up to 1,000 nits of full screen brightness with 1,600 peak brightness for HDR content. and you get a prootion 120 Hz display, which means buttery smooth scrolling. Once you get used to it, it's hard to go back. The display on the iPad Pro is hands down one of the most beautiful displays that Apple makes and one of the best displays that I've personally ever used. Trust me, videos don't do it justice. You have to pick it up in person, and you'll see what I'm talking about. It's a joy, not just to consume content, but also create content on as well. If you're a digital artist working in Procreate or editing videos in Final Cut or Lumaf Fusion, it's wonderful to see everything with such rich colors and vibrancy. Plus, that higher refresh rate means smoother strokes, less latency, and a paperlike feel, especially when paired with the Apple Pencil Pro. Now, that is not to say that the Airs display is bad. It's not bad by any means, but it is a 60 Hz display, which in 2025, it kind of feels slightly dated. And when you compare it to the Pro overall, it does fall short. And if you're someone who likes to work outdoors or near windows at coffee shops, that display just doesn't seem that bright. Whereas with the Pro, it looks beautiful. So if visuals, color accuracy, and responsiveness are critical to your workflow, then the iPad Pro's display is unmatched. Also, the iPad Pro is the only iPad that is also available with a nanoextured glass display, which drastically reduces glare and reflections and also gives you the feeling of writing on paper with the Apple Pencil. But if you slap on a nice paper-like screen protector, you can immediately replicate that for just 50 bucks on the iPad Air or even the iPad Pro if you get it with the standard glass option. And I have a link to it in the description below. All right, now let's discuss performance. And before we get into the specifics, let me just say that both the iPads, the M5 iPad Pro and the M3 iPad Air are lightning fast. We have come to that point now where for most users, you're not even really going to tell the difference between them. And for daily tasks, they're almost identical. The M3 Air can handle 4K video editing, Photoshop, and multitasking with ease. But obviously, the M5 Pro takes it a step further with more than 50% GPU performance and an improved neural engine for AI and rendering tasks. So, for creatives, the M5's 10 core GPU handles 3D modeling, Da Vinci Resolve, and Final Cut Pro exports much faster. It's also much more efficient, so even under heavy workloads, it stays relatively cool. And for students, the M3 is already overkill for note-taking, research, and multitasking between apps like Safari, Notes, and Word, for example. You can comfortably run split screen apps, stream lectures, and take handwritten notes with zero lag. In short, unless you're doing professional level video editing or graphic design at scale, the M3 in the Air is plenty. But the Pro does give you more headroom if your projects do grow more intensive down the line. But like I said, for most users, the M3 is already lightning fast. Heck, even the M2 chip is still more than enough for most people. Whether you are on an M3 or M5, you're not really going to find a difference, unless you, of course, do very graphics intensive tasks. Now, let's discuss accessories. It's perhaps one of the biggest reasons to get an iPad at all, because it makes this magical sheet of glass into a full-blown powerhouse. Both the iPad Pro and the iPad Air support the Apple Pencil Pro, which has squeeze and barrel roll gestures. Amazing for artists who need precision. The haptic feedback also makes it feel more like a real pen. For students, this is a dream for handwritten notes and diagrams. The hover feature even lets you preview strokes before you draw. Now, where things get a little different is when you look at the Magic Keyboard. Though both are designed for great typing experiences and have function row keys, a USBC port, and a can lever design, the Magic Keyboard on the iPad Pro takes things further with a premium aluminum palm rest with backlit keys, while the Magic Keyboard for the iPad Air has a more basic soft touch palm rest without haptics and without backlit keys. Typing on the iPad Pro's Magic Keyboard makes it feel more like a MacBook, but the overall key travel and experience of typing is identical to the Air. So whether you're typing essays or editing photos, both iPads support the same nextG tools.
